evals.expected_output: Identifies: C1 (wait(for:) in async context deadlock), C3 (URLSession.shared real network), H1 (static var sut shared state), H3 (Thread.sleep), H5 (UUID() and Date() non-deterministic), H6 (missing @MainActor), M5 (sut not nilled in tearDown), M6 (#expect(isValid) pre-evaluated Bool loses expression context). Also notes C2-like issue: testProcessOrder has no assertion, just a print.

Identifies all three framework mixing anti-patterns: C5 (XCTAssert in @Test silently passes - no XCTestCase context), C6 (#expect in XCTestCase silently ignored - no Swift Testing context), C7 (assertSnapshot uses XCTAssert under the hood - same as C5). Fixes: use #expect in @Test, use XCTAssert in XCTestCase, keep snapshot tests as XCTestCase until library adds Swift Testing support.

H7: test prefix + @Test causes double execution — XCTest discovers via prefix, Swift Testing via @Test. Fix: remove test prefix for Swift Testing methods (or remove @Test if keeping XCTestCase). H8: XCTest parallel ran tests in SEPARATE PROCESSES so static state was isolated. Swift Testing runs in ONE PROCESS — all parallel tests share the same static state. Fix: eliminate shared static state or add @Suite(.serialized) temporarily.

C4: force-unwrap (try!) crashes instead of failing the test. Fix: try XCTUnwrap() or try #require(). H5: Date() makes test time-dependent and non-repeatable. Fix: inject deterministic date/clock. M6: pre-evaluated Bool loses expression context for #expect. Fix: put full expression inside: #expect(EmailValidator.validate(...) == false). C2: test with no assertions always passes. Fix: add at least one #expect.

H2: only happy-path tested (checkout has no error/empty-cart/nil tests). H4: testing private method calculatePrivateDiscount directly — tests implementation not behavior. M1: cryptic names (test1, testX) don't describe what's being tested. M2: XCTFail() with no message is useless. M3: MockCart nested in the test file should be in Tests/Mocks/. M4: raw CartItem initializer instead of a test data factory (CartItem.sample()). Coverage metrics lie because coverage only measures which lines were executed, not which behaviors and edge cases were verified.
